text.title: Locomotion Options and UI Placement Rules
text.summary: Locomotion method selection with comfort ratings, UI panel placement rules, gaze-confirm interaction pattern, and spatial audio requirements.

section.title: Locomotion

rule.statement: Default to teleportation plus snap turn. Smooth locomotion and smooth turning are opt-in only, with vignette automatically enabled when the user activates them.
rule.rationale: Agents and developers default to smooth locomotion as the "modern" option; teleport must be pinned as default to protect motion-sensitive users and wheelchair users.

list.item: Teleportation (high comfort): best for general movement, seated users, and wheelchair users. Should be the default.
Walking/room-scale (high comfort for non-disabled): limited to the physical play area; excludes wheelchair users by design — always pair with teleport.
Controller/smooth movement (medium comfort): exploration use case; requires vignette and speed cap when enabled.
Flying (low-medium comfort): specific use cases only; highest nausea risk; offer vignette and speed limits.

rule.statement: Always offer a seated mode that makes every interaction reachable without standing. Test with a wheelchair-user proxy before shipping.
rule.rationale: Seated mode is the most commonly skipped accessibility feature in room-scale VR; agents will omit it without an explicit check.

section.reference: Comfort-defaults schema with locomotion, turn, vignette, UI, accessibility, and performance fields

rule.statement: Place UI panels at 1.5-3 m from the user, at eye level (sitting or standing), curved to match the viewing angle. Never head-lock any UI element.
rule.rationale: Head-locked UI causes immediate nausea because it moves with every head motion; world-locked or body-locked UI stays stable relative to the environment or the user's body.

rule.statement: Minimum text size in VR is 14-16 pt; test readability at the actual 2 m viewing distance. Web/mobile point sizes do not transfer to VR.
rule.rationale: Text that reads fine on a monitor is unreadable in a VR headset at viewing distance; agents generate text sizes from web/mobile experience and must be corrected.

rule.statement: Prefer diegetic UI (holographic displays, wrist-mounted, screens on objects) where it fits the narrative. Reserve non-diegetic UI (pause menus, settings, HUDs) for meta-level interactions with clear visual distinction from the world.
rule.rationale: Mixed diegetic and non-diegetic without clear distinction breaks presence and increases cognitive load.

rule.statement: Implement gaze-plus-confirm selection (gaze highlights the object, button press or dwell confirms) as an accessible baseline interaction that works without hand precision.
rule.rationale: Gaze selection works from a distance, is accessible to users with reduced hand mobility, and requires no controller precision.

rule.statement: Confirm every button press with a spatial audio cue and synchronized haptic feedback. Add 3D-positioned audio cues for out-of-view events.
rule.rationale: Multimodal feedback confirms interactions for users who may not see or feel a single modality; spatial audio for off-screen events prevents missing critical signals.
